title: Negative magnetoresistance in strained $α$-Sn and $α$-SnGe films in an in-plane magnetic field
authors: Sunny Phan, Andrei Kogan, Jesse Thompson, Trent Johnson, Alexander Khaetskii, Arnold Kiefer
abstract: To test the hypothesis that the chiral anomaly is responsible for negative magnetoresitance (MR) in \atn{}, we have studied magnetotransport in strained, epitaxial films of pure \aSn{} and the alloy \aSnGe{} that are in the Dirac semimetal and 3D topological insulator state, respectively. We have observed for both states a negative MR with current either parallel or transverse to the in-plane magnetic field, but with a different dependence of MR on $\vec{B}$ strength. Our results are inconsistent with the chiral anomaly and suggest that other mechanisms may be responsible for negative MR in the Dirac/Weyl semimetal phase of \aSn{}. We also discuss several factors in sample design and material quality that may be contributing to the incongruous observations of MR reported in studies of strained \atn{} films.
summary: This paper investigates the origin of negative magnetoresistance in strained alpha-Sn and alpha-SnGe films. By comparing the transport properties of the Dirac semimetal phase and the 3D topological insulator phase, the researchers demonstrate that the negative MR occurs even when the chiral anomaly is absent. This suggests that the effect is likely driven by strong spin-orbit coupling rather than the chiral anomaly. The study also highlights how material quality and phase impurities like beta-Sn can complicate the interpretation of topological transport phenomena.
main_problem: The study aims to determine if the chiral anomaly is the physical origin of negative magnetoresistance (MR) in strained alpha-Sn and alpha-SnGe films.
main_result: The authors find that negative MR persists in both the Dirac semimetal and the 3D topological insulator phase, leading them to reject the chiral anomaly as the primary mechanism and suggest a mechanism related to strong spin-orbit interaction.
method: Magnetotransport measurements were performed on MBE-grown epitaxial films at 5 K using a 9-Tesla solenoid magnet, with structural characterization via X-ray diffraction and RHEED.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of strained, epitaxial thin films of pure alpha-Sn (a Dirac semimetal) and alpha-SnGe alloy (a 3D topological insulator) grown on CdTe substrates.
key_observables: Magnetoresistance (MR), Hall resistivity, carrier density, and transport mobility.
important_parameters: Temperature of 5 K, magnetic field strength up to 8 T, and in-plane vs. out-of-plane field orientations.
main_assumptions: The films are assumed to be elastically strained with negligible relaxation, and the analysis assumes the absence of significant current shunting from the substrate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Dirac point schematic and Hall bar geometry; Figure 2 displays MR for out-of-plane and parallel magnetic fields; Figure 3 shows in-plane MR for different orientations; Figure 4 and 5 present XRD scans and reciprocal space maps for strain analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of negative MR, describes the material growth and device fabrication, presents magnetotransport results for different field orientations, discusses the rejection of the chiral anomaly hypothesis, and concludes with an analysis of material quality and potential confounding factors like beta-Sn formation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Dirac points k = (0, 0, ±kd) in a strained α-Sn film under a tensile strain normal to the film plane due to a “negative gap” between the inverted light-holes band (invLH) and the heavy-holes band (HH). b) Schematic of the Hall bar and electronics configuration for the magnetotransport measurement. The coordinate system defines directions relative to the Hall bar.

FIG. 2. a) Magnetoresistance of a strained α−Sn epilayer grown on CdTe with magnetic field Bz normal to the film plane (black trace) and B|| parallel to the current direction (blue trace), at T= 5K. b): the expanded view of the |B| < 1 T data showing negative MR when B|| ≥0.5 T .

FIG. 3. Magnetoresistance MR with the magnetic field parallel to the film plane. a) ⃗B = B∥ˆx, monotonic negative MR up to maximum field b) ⃗B = B∥ˆx, expanded view of |B| < 1.5 T c) ⃗B = B⊥ˆy, negative MR up to |B| < 5.5 T , then MR follows an increasing trend d) ⃗B = B⊥ˆy, expanded view of |B| < 1.5 T. All samples show qualitatively the same MR behavior with in-plane field and Ge-alloying slightly reduces the field effects on the samples.

FIG. 4. Out-of-plane XRD 2θ −ω scan data for sample S2. A fit using a one-layer model with homogeneous Sn and Ge concentration is overlaid. Film and substrate peaks are anno- tated. The relative peak positions indicate the film is under biaxial tensile strain.
FIG. 6. Optical micrograph of a finished Hall bar structure fabricated from an α-Sn film on CdTe. Feature dimensions are in the main text.

FIG. 5. Reciprocal space maps of the a) (004), and b) (335) Bragg reflections for sample S2 plotted in dimensionless recip- rocal lattice units (rlu) on a logarithmic intensity scale. Film and substrate peaks are annotated.

FIG. 7. Magnetoresistance of strained α-Sn and α-SnGe with Bz normal to the films’ plane. All samples shows qualitatively the same field dependence with MR ≈400% at maximum field and Ge-alloying slightly reduces the field effect.

FIG. 8. Black trace: measured Hall resistivity of Ge-alloyed α-Sn with magnetic field B normal to the film plane. Red trace: linear fit, up to B = 1 T. The negative slope corre- sponds to n-type carriers. a) Sample S2, b) Sample S3.
